mnemonics.title: OPCODE = Operation Code Defines Operation Direction
mnemonics.content: OPCODE tells the CPU WHAT to do — it's the 'verb' of the instruction that defines the operation to be performed (ADD, SUB, LOAD, STORE, etc.)

mnemonics.title: Address Fields Are The Nouns
mnemonics.content: Address fields specify WHERE the data is — they're the 'nouns' that tell the CPU where to find (source) or put (destination) the data

mnemonics.title: 0-1-2-3 Stack Path
mnemonics.content: Types of instruction formats by number of addresses: 0-Address (Stack machines), 1-Address (Accumulator based), 2-Address, 3-Address — Think '0123' from zero to three addresses

mnemonics.title: DADD TADD SAD — Data Transfer, Arithmetic, Control
mnemonics.content: Main instruction types: Data Transfer (move data), Data Processing/Arithmetic (operate on data), and Control Transfer (change flow) — DADD TADD SAD helps remember the order

mnemonics.title: Direct Goes Straight, Indirect Takes A Roundabout
mnemonics.content: Direct addressing: address field contains the actual memory address. Indirect addressing: address field contains a pointer to the memory address — Direct = straight path, Indirect = two-step lookup

acronyms.usage: The field that specifies the operation to be performed by the CPU (e.g., ADD, MOV, JMP)
Holds the currently executing instruction; receives instruction from memory via MAR/MBR
Holds the address of the next instruction to be fetched; increments after each fetch
Holds the memory address from which data is to be fetched or to which data is to be stored
Holds the data to be written to memory or the data just read from memory
Performs arithmetic and logical operations as specified by the opcode

visualTips.visualization: Draw a rectangular box divided into sections like a train car: the first compartment is OPCODE (the engine), followed by address field compartments (the cargo cars). For a 3-address instruction: [OPCODE][Address 1][Address 2][Address 3]
Picture a 3-step assembly line: FETCH = going to the shelf (memory) to get the instruction, DECODE = reading the instructions, EXECUTE = actually doing the work
Direct = house number written on a letter (goes straight there). Indirect = a PO Box number written on the letter (need to go to PO Box first to get the actual address)
The value is IN the instruction itself — like having a handwritten note with the number already written on it, no need to look anywhere else
Think of registers as the CPU's 'workbench' — fastest access location. Like having tools on your desk vs having to go to a storage room (memory)

analogies.analogy: An instruction is like a recipe: OPCODE is the cooking verb (fry, boil, bake), address fields are the ingredient locations (pantry, fridge, shelf). The CPU follows the recipe step by step.

An instruction format is like a standardized form: just as a bank form has fields for name, account number, amount, an instruction has fields for opcode, source address, destination address — each field has a specific purpose and position.
The Program Counter is like a bookmark in a book — it remembers which page (instruction address) to read next, and you move it forward after each instruction.

analogies.concept: IR As The Chef's Current Recipe
analogies.analogy: The Instruction Register is like the recipe card the chef is currently reading and cooking from — it's holding one instruction at a time, being actively processed.

Addressing modes are like different ways to find a book: Register (on your desk) = instant, Memory (in library) = walk there, Immediate (in your hand) = already have it. Speed decreases in that order.
